methodsAttempted.query: site:wustl.edu Washington University in St. Louis generative AI policy students teaching ChatGPT academic integrity
methodsAttempted.notes: Firecrawl search found the CTL student guide, the WashU+AI hub, CTL course-policy language, CTL generative-AI teaching guidance, and a WashU IT DeepSeek advisory.

methodsAttempted.query: site:wustl.edu WashU generative AI approved tools privacy data DeepSeek ChatGPT Enterprise policy
methodsAttempted.notes: Firecrawl search surfaced the WashU IT DeepSeek advisory as the main official security/privacy guidance result for that query.
Firecrawl search found the official WashU IT Generative Artificial Intelligence page with AI tools, sensitive-data guidance, and AI purchasing guidance.
Verified it.wustl.edu and ctl.wustl.edu as Washington University in St. Louis official subdomains; ai.wustl.edu redirected to the official WashU AI hub at ai.washu.edu.

The IT AI page links an external Smartsheet AI Application Chart; it was documented but not used as direct policy evidence because the official IT page provides enough source text on the approved-tool process.

artifacts.summary: Official-source discovery found WashU IT, WashU+AI, and WashU Center for Teaching and Learning pages with substantive generative-AI guidance. Approved claims are limited to those official sources: secure-tool and sensitive-data guidance, student responsibility to clarify course expectations, academic-integrity/citation guidance, course-policy options, vendor-security review for unapproved tools, and a narrow DeepSeek advisory. The target QS ranking metadata was not used as policy evidence.

artifacts.claimText: WashU IT guidance says users should not enter Washington University or secure data, including deidentified healthcare data, into publicly accessible non-protected AI tools.

citation.citationTitle: Washington University in St. Louis AI Policy Tracker record
citation.publisher: Washington University in St. Louis Information Technology
citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) - Information Technology
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: The university supports and encourages the responsible and secure exploration of AI tools. When using any publicly accessible, non-protected AI tools, it is vitally important that you do not enter any Washington University or secure data, including deidentified healthcare data of any kind, into these platforms.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: WashU IT warns users not to enter university or secure data into public non-protected AI tools.

citation.citationTitle: Washington University in St. Louis AI Policy Tracker record
citation.publisher: Washington University in St. Louis Center for Teaching and Learning
citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.sourceTitle: ChatGPT and other AI Composition Tools: A Guide for Students
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: In regard to your classwork, please check with your instructor and the syllabus about whether and how you can use ChatGPT and other tools to help you with your classwork. Ask the instructor how AI tools are or are not to be used in the class. There is no set policy across instructors as they are also figuring out how AI fits into the future of their disciplines and how best to teach you the skills needed for future professions. If there is no explicit policy outlined, it is better to assume that AI use is banned.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: The CTL student guide says students should check their instructor and syllabus, and assume AI use is banned if no explicit policy is outlined.
artifacts.evidenceLocator: The Use of ChatGPT in Academic Settings
artifacts.rightsNote: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.claimText: WashU CTL student guidance says students should not represent AI-tool output as their own work and should cite AI contributions when using AI tools.

citation.citationTitle: Washington University in St. Louis AI Policy Tracker record
citation.publisher: Washington University in St. Louis Center for Teaching and Learning
citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.sourceTitle: ChatGPT and other AI Composition Tools: A Guide for Students
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Never take the output from AI tools and represent it as your own work. This not only violates the Terms of Use for most AI tools, creating a legal issue, but is also clearly and unarguably plagiarism. To avoid plagiarism when you use AI tools, make sure you cite its contribution accordingly when you use it.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: The CTL guide warns not to submit AI output as one's own work and tells students to cite AI contributions.
artifacts.rightsNote: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Language for Course Policies on Artificial Intelligence (AI)
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: There are many possible approaches to policies on the use of GenAI in a course. Your policies can also vary within a course by assignment or assignment type. The sections below describe potential policy types and offer examples of syllabus language for each type. AI Usage Level: Allowed Without Restriction; Allowed With Citations; Restricted Partially; Restricted Completely.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: The CTL page frames course AI policy as instructor-set and lists four possible policy categories.

artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: We have evaluated DeepSeek, which is an open-source large language model (LLM) and have determined it is not safe for use with university non-public information. artifacts.title: Washington University in St. Louis staged generative-AI guidance evidence
artifacts.summary: Staged evidence for Washington University in St. Louis uses official WashU IT, WashU+AI, and Center for Teaching and Learning pages. The approved claims are limited to official-source statements about sensitive-data handling in public AI tools, secure AI tools approved for HIPAA/FERPA data, student responsibility to clarify course AI expectations, AI plagiarism and citation guidance, CTL course-policy categories, OIS Vendor Security Review for unapproved AI tools, and a narrow DeepSeek non-public-information advisory.
limitations: This tracker is not legal advice, not academic integrity advice, and not an official university statement unless a linked source is the university's own official page.
Claims are scoped to the official WashU pages cited and should not be read as a complete university-wide policy code.
CTL course-policy examples are teaching guidance and were not converted into binding university-wide rules.
The external Smartsheet AI Application Chart was documented but not used as direct evidence in this run.
QS row metadata was target metadata only and was not used as policy evidence.
artifacts.sourceRightsPolicy: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.
